children.value:
The rules of section 6.2 allow us to differentiate any sum or scalar multiple
of elementary functions, and together with the power rule they make every
polynomial routine. But functions in mechanical engineering are rarely just
sums. The position of a damped oscillator, for example, is a product of an
exponential decay and a cosine oscillation: two non-trivial functions multiplied
together. No combination of the sum and factor rules can handle such an
expression. We need a rule for products, and a companion rule for quotients.

children.html: (f⋅g)′=f′⋅g+f⋅g′\bigl(f \cdot g\bigr)' = f' \cdot g + f \cdot g'(f⋅g)′=f′⋅g+f⋅g′

children.value: and can apply it to products of elementary functions.

children.html: (fg)′=f′⋅g−f⋅g′g2\left(\dfrac{f}{g}\right)' = \dfrac{f' \cdot g - f \cdot g'}{g^2}(gf​)′=g2f′⋅g−f⋅g′​
g≠0g \neq 0g=0

children.value:
You can identify when the product or quotient rule is needed and carry
out the differentiation correctly, including simplification of the result.
How does a damped oscillator move?

children.label: How does a damped oscillator move?

children.value:
A mass attached to a spring and a viscous damper is one of the most
fundamental models in mechanical engineering. The spring pulls the mass back
toward equilibrium with a force proportional to the displacement; the damper
resists motion with a force proportional to the velocity and converts kinetic
energy into heat. When the mass is displaced and released, it does not
oscillate indefinitely: the amplitude shrinks with every cycle as the damper
dissipates energy.

children.alt:
A mass-spring-damper system. The mass m is displaced from its equilibrium
position and released. The resulting motion is a damped oscillation.
(Source: own figure; licence CC BY-SA
4.0)

children.html: mmm

children.value:
is displaced from its equilibrium
position and released. The resulting motion is a damped oscillation.
(Source: own figure; licence

children.value: How fast is the mass moving, and in which direction?

children.html: v(t)=x′(t)v(t) = x'(t)v(t)=x′(t)

children.value:
is what we want, and computing it requires differentiating a product of two
functions. Neither the marble track nor any polynomial from sections 6.1 and
6.2 prepared us for this: we need a new rule.
We apply the limit definition directly to the product

children.html: f(x)⋅g(x)f(x)\cdot g(x)f(x)⋅g(x)

children.value:
. The
standard approach is to add and subtract a bridge term

children.html: f(x) g(x+h)f(x)\,g(x+h)f(x)g(x+h)

children.value:
in the
numerator, which allows the difference quotient to be split into two
recognisable pieces:

children.html: (f⋅g)′(x)=lim⁡h→0f(x+h) g(x+h)−f(x) g(x)h=lim⁡h→0f(x+h) g(x+h)−f(x) g(x+h)+f(x) g(x+h)−f(x) g(x)h=lim⁡h→0f(x+h)−f(x)h⋅g(x+h)  +  f(x)⋅lim⁡h→0g(x+h)−g(x)h.\begin{align*} \bigl(f \cdot g\bigr)'(x) &= \lim_{h \to 0} \frac{f(x+h)\,g(x+h) - f(x)\,g(x)}{h} \\[4pt] &= \lim_{h \to 0} \frac{f(x+h)\,g(x+h) - f(x)\,g(x+h) + f(x)\,g(x+h) - f(x)\,g(x)}{h} \\[4pt] &= \lim_{h \to 0} \frac{f(x+h) - f(x)}{h}\cdot g(x+h) \;+\; f(x)\cdot \lim_{h \to 0} \frac{g(x+h) - g(x)}{h}. \end{align*}(f⋅g)′(x)​=h→0lim​hf(x+h)g(x+h)−f(x)g(x)​=h→0lim​hf(x+h)g(x+h)−f(x)g(x+h)+f(x)g(x+h)−f(x)g(x)​=h→0lim​hf(x+h)−f(x)​⋅g(x+h)+f(x)⋅h→0lim​hg(x+h)−g(x)​.​
h→0h \to 0h→0
f′(x)f'(x)f′(x)
g(x+h)g(x + h)g(x+h)
g(x)g(x)g(x)

children.value: by continuity. The second ratio approaches

children.html: g′(x)g'(x)g′(x)
(f⋅g)′=f′⋅g+f⋅g′.\bigl(f \cdot g\bigr)' = f' \cdot g + f \cdot g'.(f⋅g)′=f′⋅g+f⋅g′.
fff
ggg
(f(x)⋅g(x))′=f′(x)⋅g(x)+f(x)⋅g′(x).\bigl(f(x) \cdot g(x)\bigr)' = f'(x) \cdot g(x) + f(x) \cdot g'(x).(f(x)⋅g(x))′=f′(x)⋅g(x)+f(x)⋅g′(x).

children.value:
A useful way to read this rule aloud is: derivative of the first times the
second, plus the first times the derivative of the second. The two terms
capture the independent contribution of each factor to the overall rate of
change.
Notice that the rule is not symmetric:

children.html: (f⋅g)′≠f′⋅g′(f \cdot g)' \neq f' \cdot g'(f⋅g)′=f′⋅g′

children.value:
.
The product of the derivatives is not the derivative of the product, just as
the composition of two functions is not the composition of their derivatives,
as we discussed in section 3.6.
What is the velocity of the damped oscillator?

children.label: What is the velocity of the damped oscillator?

children.value: We apply the product rule to

children.html: x(t)=A e−δtcos⁡(ωd t)x(t) = A\,e^{-\delta t}\cos(\omega_d\,t)x(t)=Ae−δtcos(ωd​t)
f(t)=A e−δtf(t) = A\,e^{-\delta t}f(t)=Ae−δt
g(t)=cos⁡(ωd t)g(t) = \cos(\omega_d\,t)g(t)=cos(ωd​t)

children.value: The derivatives of these two factors each involve a composition:

children.html: e−δte^{-\delta t}e−δt
eue^ueu
u=−δtu = -\delta tu=−δt
cos⁡(ωd t)\cos(\omega_d\,t)cos(ωd​t)
cos⁡(u)\cos(u)cos(u)
u=ωd tu = \omega_d\,tu=ωd​t

children.value:
. As section 6.4 will confirm using the chain
rule, their derivatives are

children.html: f′(t)=−Aδ e−δt,g′(t)=−ωdsin⁡(ωd t).f'(t) = -A\delta\,e^{-\delta t}, \qquad g'(t) = -\omega_d\sin(\omega_d\,t).f′(t)=−Aδe−δt,g′(t)=−ωd​sin(ωd​t).

children.value: The product rule then gives the velocity:

children.html: v(t)=x′(t)=f′(t) g(t)+f(t) g′(t)=−Aδ e−δtcos⁡(ωd t)−Aωd e−δtsin⁡(ωd t)=−A e−δt(δcos⁡(ωd t)+ωdsin⁡(ωd t)).\begin{align*} v(t) = x'(t) &= f'(t)\,g(t) + f(t)\,g'(t) \\ &= -A\delta\,e^{-\delta t}\cos(\omega_d\,t) - A\omega_d\,e^{-\delta t}\sin(\omega_d\,t) \\ &= -A\,e^{-\delta t}\bigl(\delta\cos(\omega_d\,t) + \omega_d\sin(\omega_d\,t)\bigr). \end{align*}v(t)=x′(t)​=f′(t)g(t)+f(t)g′(t)=−Aδe−δtcos(ωd​t)−Aωd​e−δtsin(ωd​t)=−Ae−δt(δcos(ωd​t)+ωd​sin(ωd​t)).​

children.value: The velocity is itself a product of the decaying envelope

children.html: e−δte^{-\delta t}e−δt

children.value:
and
an oscillating term, so it also decays to zero as

children.html: t→∞t \to \inftyt→∞

children.value:
, as expected
for a system losing energy continuously.

children.html: 0.05 m/s0.05~\text{m/s}0.05 m/s

children.value:
back toward
equilibrium. The negative sign confirms the direction: the mass starts at
positive displacement

children.html: x(0)=0.10 mx(0) = 0.10~\text{m}x(0)=0.10 m

children.value:
and the restoring force is
already pulling it inward, so the initial velocity is indeed directed toward
the origin.

Products and quotients are closely related, and the quotient rule can be
derived directly from the product rule without returning to the limit
definition. Let

children.html: q(x)=f(x)/g(x)q(x) = f(x)/g(x)q(x)=f(x)/g(x)
f(x)=q(x)⋅g(x)f(x) = q(x)\cdot g(x)f(x)=q(x)⋅g(x)

children.value:
.
Differentiating both sides with the product rule:

children.html: f′(x)=q′(x)⋅g(x)+q(x)⋅g′(x).f'(x) = q'(x)\cdot g(x) + q(x)\cdot g'(x).f′(x)=q′(x)⋅g(x)+q(x)⋅g′(x).
q′(x)q'(x)q′(x)
q(x)=f(x)/g(x)q(x) = f(x)/g(x)q(x)=f(x)/g(x)

q′(x)=f′(x)−q(x)⋅g′(x)g(x)=f′(x)−f(x)g(x)⋅g′(x)g(x)=f′(x) g(x)−f(x) g′(x)g(x)2.q'(x) = \frac{f'(x) - q(x)\cdot g'(x)}{g(x)} = \frac{f'(x) - \dfrac{f(x)}{g(x)}\cdot g'(x)}{g(x)} = \frac{f'(x)\,g(x) - f(x)\,g'(x)}{g(x)^2}.q′(x)=g(x)f′(x)−q(x)⋅g′(x)​=g(x)f′(x)−g(x)f(x)​⋅g′(x)​=g(x)2f′(x)g(x)−f(x)g′(x)​.
fff
ggg
g(x)≠0g(x) \neq 0g(x)=0
(f(x)g(x))′=f′(x) g(x)−f(x) g′(x)g(x)2.\left(\frac{f(x)}{g(x)}\right)' = \frac{f'(x)\,g(x) - f(x)\,g'(x)}{g(x)^2}.(g(x)f(x)​)′=g(x)2f′(x)g(x)−f(x)g′(x)​.

children.value: The order of terms in the numerator matters: it is always

children.html: f′gf'gf′g
fg′f g'fg′

children.value:
,
never the other way around. A sign error here produces a result that is wrong
by a sign and a factor of two, so it is worth double-checking by substituting
a simple example.
Can we verify the derivative of the tangent?

children.label: Can we verify the derivative of the tangent?

children.value: In section 6.1 we stated without proof that

children.html: (tan⁡x)′=1/cos⁡2x(\tan x)' = 1/\cos^2 x(tanx)′=1/cos2x

children.value:
. The
quotient rule now lets us derive this result. We write

children.html: tan⁡x=sin⁡x/cos⁡x\tan x = \sin x / \cos xtanx=sinx/cosx
f(x)=sin⁡xf(x) = \sin xf(x)=sinx
g(x)=cos⁡xg(x) = \cos xg(x)=cosx
f′(x)=cos⁡xf'(x) = \cos xf′(x)=cosx
g′(x)=−sin⁡xg'(x) = -\sin xg′(x)=−sinx

children.value:
(\tan x)' = \frac{\cos x \cdot \cos x - \sin x \cdot (-\sin x)}{\cos^2 x}
          = \frac{\cos^2 x + \sin^2 x}{\cos^2 x}.
children.html: (tan⁡x)′=cos⁡x⋅cos⁡x−sin⁡x⋅(−sin⁡x)cos⁡2x=cos⁡2x+sin⁡2xcos⁡2x.(\tan x)' = \frac{\cos x \cdot \cos x - \sin x \cdot (-\sin x)}{\cos^2 x} = \frac{\cos^2 x + \sin^2 x}{\cos^2 x}.(tanx)′=cos2xcosx⋅cosx−sinx⋅(−sinx)​=cos2xcos2x+sin2x​.

children.value: The numerator is exactly the Pythagorean identity from section 4.5:

children.html: cos⁡2x+sin⁡2x=1\cos^2 x + \sin^2 x = 1cos2x+sin2x=1
(tan⁡x)′=1cos⁡2x,(\tan x)' = \frac{1}{\cos^2 x},(tanx)′=cos2x1​,

children.value:
confirming the entry in the reference table of section 6.1. The Pythagorean
identity, which we proved geometrically from the unit circle, turns out to
play an algebraic role in differential calculus as well.

The product rule and the quotient rule complete the toolkit for differentiating
algebraic combinations of functions. Applied to the damped oscillator, the
product rule produces the velocity in a direct calculation, and the result
decays to zero as expected from the physical model. The quotient rule,
derived from the product rule without a separate limit argument, closes the
open item from section 6.1 by giving a clean derivation of

children.html: (tan⁡x)′=1/cos⁡2x(\tan x)' = 1/\cos^2 x(tanx)′=1/cos2x

children.value:
.
Both rules required knowing the derivatives of the individual factors, and in
the oscillator example those factors were composite functions whose derivatives
we used without yet justifying them. Providing that justification is the task
of the next section, where the chain rule handles any composition of
differentiable functions.
